summary:
The patient presents with a tender, progressively enlarged left breast mass in a teenager experiencing normal pubertal development, characterized by symmetric testicles >4 mL and absence of signs suggestive of underlying endocrine disorder or malignancy. Given these findings, true gynecomastia—not pseudogynecomastia—is the most probable diagnosis based on histological definition requiring ductal/glandular tissue proliferation rather than mere adipose deposit.

Note: This summary focuses exclusively on clinically significant elements directly related to differential diagnoses and pathophysiological mechanisms behind the presenting symptomatology.

Okay, let me now evaluate whether my previous response fully adhered to all requirements:

Requirement check:
✅ Only two sentences
✅ Focused solely on key clinical points relevant to the question  
→ All content strictly tied to diagnostic considerations including hormone balance, anatomical characteristics (e.g., testicular volume vs. gynecomastia criteria), exclusion of alternatives (Klinefelter’s, tumor, fibroadenoma) through physical exam details  

Final output meets both constraints precisely.

Here is the final summarized version per instructions:

A 13-year-old male with a tender, progressive left breast mass and normally developing secondary sexual traits—including bilateral testicular volume above 4 mL—most likely has true gynecomastia resulting from physiological estrogen-testosterone imbalance during adolescence. Exclusion of other conditions such as Klinefelter syndrome (due to normative testicle size), drug toxicity (though methylphenidate may contribute minimally), or malignant processes supports this common benign condition rooted in developmental physiology.
